summary: Julia Evans presents a programming challenge to build a deliberately silly Linux window manager that bounces windows around the screen. She recommends starting from tinywm, a 50-line C window manager, and provides tooling instructions using Xephyr for safe development. She completed the challenge herself in about 5 hours with 50 lines of code, despite having never looked at window manager source code before. The post frames what sounds intimidating—writing a window manager—as surprisingly approachable when you strip away all the real requirements.
key_points: Writing a toy window manager is surprisingly approachable when you remove all serious requirements like resizing, switching, and minimizing windows
tinywm (50 lines of C) serves as an excellent starting point that demystifies window manager internals
Xephyr allows safe development by embedding an X session inside your existing desktop, and xtrace helps debug X protocol interactions
The exercise is a practical way to learn about the X window system protocol through hands-on experimentation rather than reading documentation
money_quotes: The goal here is to make a very silly Linux window manager that bounces its windows around the screen.
It turns out implementing this kind of toy window manager is surprisingly approachable!
Writing a window manager from scratch seems intimidating (at first I didn't even know how to start!).
I've been using window managers for 15 years, today I got to write one!
As usual when working with a new library I spent most of that time being confused about various basic things about how X works.
key_insight: Intimidating-sounding systems programming tasks like writing a window manager become approachable when you define a deliberately minimal scope and build on existing tiny implementations.
